metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Indigenous Studies 11 provides a broad introduction to the histories, cultures, governance systems, and contemporary realities of Indigenous peoples in Canada and globally. Students examine the diversity of Indigenous nations and their relationships to land and community, explore the impacts of colonization, and engage with Indigenous knowledge systems as sophisticated and valid ways of understanding the world. The course privileges Indigenous voices and perspectives, and develops students' understanding of their own roles in reconciliation. It may be taken alongside or in lieu of BC First Peoples 11.
metadata.general_description: An introductory course exploring Indigenous histories, cultures, governance, and contemporary realities in Canada and globally — developing intercultural understanding and a commitment to reconciliation.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous peoples have distinct histories, cultures, and governance systems that predate and persist beyond colonization.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students explore the depth and diversity of Indigenous civilizations — their governance, knowledge systems, economies, and cultural practices — recognizing these as sophisticated and enduring systems, not merely historical artifacts.

big_ideas.statement: Colonization has profoundly shaped — and continues to shape — the lives of Indigenous peoples.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine the mechanisms and ongoing legacy of colonization — including land dispossession, assimilation policies, and systemic racism — and connect historical policies to contemporary inequities.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous knowledge systems offer valuable perspectives on sustainable relationships between people and the land.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage with Indigenous ecological knowledge, relational worldviews, and land-based ways of knowing as valid and important systems of understanding — complementary to and distinct from Western scientific frameworks.

big_ideas.statement: Reconciliation is an ongoing process requiring learning, relationship-building, and structural change.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop a nuanced understanding of what reconciliation requires — from personal learning and relationships to institutional reform and systemic change — and reflect on their own role.

competencies.raw: Apply critical and historical thinking to analyze Indigenous histories and contemporary realities; use Indigenous ways of knowing as valid frameworks for understanding; compare and contrast diverse Indigenous cultural and governance traditions.
i_can_statements: I can apply critical and historical thinking to analyze the causes and consequences of colonization for Indigenous peoples.
I can recognize and apply Indigenous knowledge frameworks as valid and sophisticated ways of understanding the world.
I can compare the histories and cultures of diverse Indigenous nations while respecting their distinctiveness.
I can evaluate contemporary Indigenous issues using evidence, diverse perspectives, and ethical reasoning.

competencies.raw: Communicate respectfully and accurately about Indigenous peoples, cultures, and issues; center Indigenous voices in discussions and written work; use nation-specific and culturally accurate language.
i_can_statements: I can communicate respectfully and accurately about Indigenous peoples using culturally appropriate language.
I can centre Indigenous voices and perspectives in my research, discussions, and written work.
I can distinguish between pan-Indigenous generalizations and nation-specific realities.
I can engage in dialogue about difficult topics — colonization, racism, reconciliation — with sensitivity and honesty.

competencies.raw: Recognize the ongoing impacts of colonization and commit to reconciliation; engage respectfully with Indigenous communities and knowledge holders; take action to challenge stereotypes and promote Indigenous rights.
i_can_statements: I can recognize systemic injustices affecting Indigenous peoples and connect them to historical policies.
I can articulate specific commitments I will make to support reconciliation in my daily life.
I can challenge stereotypes and misinformation about Indigenous peoples with accurate, respectful information.
I can engage respectfully with Indigenous communities, protocols, and Elders.

areas.name: Indigenous Diversity and Cultures
areas.raw: The diversity of Indigenous peoples, nations, and cultural traditions in Canada and globally.
topics: First Nations diversity in Canada: over 630 First Nations across 10 provinces and territories
Métis and Inuit peoples: distinct histories, cultures, and contemporary realities
Cultural regions: ways environment and territory shape distinct cultural practices
Languages as living knowledge: the relationship between language, land, and Indigenous identity
Traditional governance: clan systems, hereditary leadership, treaty-making, and consensus processes
Global Indigenous peoples: connections and shared experiences across continents

areas.name: Indigenous Knowledge Systems
areas.raw: Traditional ecological knowledge, relational worldviews, and Indigenous ways of knowing.
topics: Indigenous worldviews: relational, holistic, and land-based ways of understanding life
Traditional ecological knowledge (TEK): sustainable land and resource management practices
Oral traditions as knowledge systems: story, ceremony, and intergenerational knowledge transfer
Seasonal and cyclical knowledge: understanding time, ecology, and community through Indigenous calendars
Two-Eyed Seeing: integrating Indigenous and Western knowledge systems respectfully
Intellectual property and cultural protocols: respectful engagement with Indigenous knowledge

areas.name: Colonization in Canada
areas.raw: Colonial policies, their mechanisms, and ongoing impacts on Indigenous peoples.
topics: European contact and early colonialism: fur trade, missions, and the transformation of Indigenous life
Treaties: numbered treaties, peace and friendship treaties, and their ongoing legal significance
The Indian Act: provisions, impacts, and ongoing colonial governance
Residential schools: system, survivor testimony, TRC findings, and the call for action
Sixties Scoop and child welfare: removal of Indigenous children and intergenerational impacts
Ongoing colonialism: land dispossession, resource extraction, and systemic racism

areas.name: Resilience and Self-Determination
areas.raw: Indigenous resistance, cultural revitalization, and contemporary self-determination.
topics: Indigenous resistance: historical and contemporary forms from legal challenges to land defense
Cultural revitalization: language nests, land-based education, art, and ceremony renewal
Section 35 and Indigenous rights: constitutional protections and their judicial interpretation
Modern land claims and self-government: paths toward political and territorial sovereignty
Indigenous economies: sustainable business development, land stewardship, and economic sovereignty
Indigenous youth leadership: young people at the forefront of reclamation and advocacy

areas.name: Reconciliation
areas.raw: Truth and Reconciliation, UNDRIP, and pathways to just relationships.
topics: Truth and Reconciliation Commission: mandate, process, findings, and 94 Calls to Action
UNDRIP: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples and implementation in Canada/BC
MMIWG Inquiry: National Inquiry findings, calls for justice, and community responses
What reconciliation requires: government responsibilities, institutional change, and individual action
Allyship and solidarity: how non-Indigenous people can support Indigenous rights and well-being
Indigenous and non-Indigenous partnerships: examples of transformative relationship-building
